text: Synthetic nonheme iron enzymes can catalyze the dioxygenation of numerous substrates through the generation of a high-spin iron(IV)=O species. However, the true enzyme-like activation of O 2 to produce a high-spin iron(IV)=O species remains an elusive goal in synthetic chemistry. Here, we demonstrate the activation of O 2 to generate a high-spin iron(IV)=O species at the iron(II) centers of a metal–organic framework, at low temperatures, using the same local geometry present in α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases. The formation of the iron(IV)=O species is confirmed through in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform, in situ and variable-field Mössbauer, Fe Kβ x-ray emission, and nuclear resonance vibrational spectroscopies. Upon exposure to O 2 the catalyst facilitates the oxygenation of cyclohexane and the stoichiometric oxidation of ethane to ethanol.
